coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence determination of carbon disulfide in water using surface functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Ruoyu
Chen, Xipao
Hu, Yaoping
coredata.dc:description: Trace level of CS2 in water causes a serious threat to the environment and living organisms. Thus, the development of reliable methods for its sensitive detection is quite necessary. Herein, we design a dual-emission platform composed of blue and yellow emissive carbon dots (B-CDs and Y-CDs) for ratiometric fluorescence determination of CS2. B-CDs, serving as the indicator, are functionalized by hydrophilic polyethylenimine groups on the surface, which can react with CS2 to produce hydrophobic dithiocarbamate moieties, resulting in the aggregation of nanoparticles and quenching of blue fluorescence. In contrast, Y-CDs without polyethylenimine groups exhibit no reactivity toward CS2 and act as the internal reference. With the increase of CS2 concentration, the blue fluorescence of the sensing platform at 461 nm continually drops, but the yellow fluorescence at 560 nm keeps nearly constant. Correspondingly, the vivid color change from blue-white to yellow can be observed by the naked eyes, realizing the visual detection. There is a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I461/I560) and CS2 concentration in the range of 7.7–1400 μM. The detection limit is as low as 2.3 μM. Furthermore, the sensing platform is successfully applied to monitor CS2 in real water, validating its good practicability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon disulfide
Visual detection
Carbon dots
Polyethylenimine groups

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of B-CDs and Y-CDs
Fluorescence response toward CS2 and the sensing mechanism
Detection of CS2 in real water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LQ20E020005 ), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science ( SKLACLS2004 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City ( 2021J075 ), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of B-CDs (a) and Y-CDs (b). Insets in (a) and (b): size distributions of B-CDs and Y-CDs. (c) FTIR, (d) XPS scan, (e) C1 s, and (f) N1 s spectra of B-CDs and Y-CDs.
UV-Vis absorption and PL spectra of B-CDs (a) and Y-CDs (b). Insets: photographs of the aqueous dispersion of B-CDs and Y-CDs under sunlight and UV light.

(a) PL spectra of B-CDs with and without CS2. Insets in (a): photographs of the aqueous dispersion of B-CDs with (left) and without (right) CS2. The concentration of CS2 is 1.5 mM. (b) PL spectra of Y-CDs with and without CS2. (c) TEM image of B-CDs after addition of CS2. (d) FTIR spectra of B-CDs and the product of B-CDs/CS2. (e) XPS S2p spectra of B-CDs and the product of B-CDs/CS2. (f) PL decay profiles of B-CDs before and after addition of CS2.

(a) PL spectrum of the phosphate-buffered solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) containing B-CDs (10 mg L–1) and Y-CDs (10 mg L–1). The excitation wavelength is set at 365 nm. (b) PL spectra of the CD mixture upon addition of different concentrations of CS2 (from top to bottom: 0, 20, 60, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 2000 μM). Inset in (b): photographs of the CD mixture upon addition of CS2 under a UV lamp (from left to right: the concentrations of CS2 are 0, 800, 1400 and 2000 μM). (c) A linear relationship between I461/I560 and the concentration of CS2 in the range of 0–1400 μM. (d) Selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform toward CS2 and other substances. The concentrations of all the analytes are 1 mM. The concentrations of the masking reagents K4P2O7 and NH4HF2 are 2 mM and 1 mM, respectively.
A schematic of ratiometric fluorescence sensing system consisting of blue and yellow emissive carbon dots (B-CDs and Y-CDs) for detection of CS2 in water.
Results of CS2 detection in real water using the CD-based sensing platform and GC.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence determination of carbon disulfide in water using surface functionalized carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Mass Spectrometry and Molecular Analysis of Zhejiang Province, School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace level of CS2 in water causes a serious threat to the environment and living organisms. Thus, the development of reliable methods for its sensitive detection is quite necessary. Herein, we design a dual-emission platform composed of blue and yellow emissive carbon dots (B-CDs and Y-CDs) for ratiometric fluorescence determination of CS2. B-CDs, serving as the indicator, are functionalized by hydrophilic polyethylenimine groups on the surface, which can react with CS2 to produce hydrophobic dithiocarbamate moieties, resulting in the aggregation of nanoparticles and quenching of blue fluorescence. In contrast, Y-CDs without polyethylenimine groups exhibit no reactivity toward CS2 and act as the internal reference. With the increase of CS2 concentration, the blue fluorescence of the sensing platform at 461 nm continually drops, but the yellow fluorescence at 560 nm keeps nearly constant. Correspondingly, the vivid color change from blue-white to yellow can be observed by the naked eyes, realizing the visual detection. There is a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I461/I560) and CS2 concentration in the range of 7.7–1400 μM. The detection limit is as low as 2.3 μM. Furthermore, the sensing platform is successfully applied to monitor CS2 in real water, validating its good practicability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon disulfide (CS2), a hazardous sulfur compound, is extensively used as a raw material or a solvent in a variety of industrial processes to manufacture rubbers, resins, and fibers, extract oil, and produce organic reagents such as carbon tetrachloride [1]. Additionally, CS2 is often applied as an effective insecticide to protect crops from pests [2]. Apart from anthropogenic release, the generation of CS2 is also associated with natural activities including volcano eruptions and forest fires [3]. In some countries and regions, CS2 heavily exists not only in the atmosphere but also in the environmental water. Excessive intake of CS2, unfortunately, can cause some serious diseases like atherosclerosis and neurological dysfunction [4]. The maximum level of CS2 in drinking water is permitted to be 26 μM for the guarantee of human health [5].

Over the past decades, various analytical techniques have been developed for the determination of CS2 in solution, mainly including gas chromatography with electron capture detection (GC/ECD) [6], gas chromatography with flame photometric detection (GC/FPD) [7], chemiluminescence assay [8], kinetic-spectrophotometric method [9], and fluorescence approach [5,10,11]. The most widely used GC methods are highly sensitive toward CS2, but they require expensive and complicated instruments, need trained operators, and are time-consuming. Alternatively, fluorescence approach has attracted much attention due to the advantages of rapid response and easy operation. However, the reported fluorescence approaches for CS2 sensing were typically based on the record of a single emission signal, which might be interfered by the instrumental and environmental factors [12]. Ratiometric fluorescence method can overcome the above drawbacks and improve the sensitivity, because it offers intrinsic self-calibration by measuring the ratio of two emission signals at different wavelengths under a single excitation [13–17]. A pioneering work conducted by Lu et al. reported the fabrication of self-assembled micelles of pyrene-modified amphiphilic hyperbranched polyethylenimine polymers which enable ratiometric fluorescence determination of CS2 based on a quenching mechanism via photoinduced electron transfer [5]. Nowadays, ratiometric fluorescence determination of CS2 is still challenging mainly due to the difficulty in design of smart fluorescent probes.

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanosized carbonaceous particles with attractive features such as tunable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, high chemical stability, simple preparation, and easy surface functionalization [18,19]. These merits make CDs promising in the fields of sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, optoelectronics, and energy storage [20,21]. Despite the great advances achieved in CD-based sensing of inorganic ions, organic explosive residues, and biomolecules [22–27], trace-level detection of the environmental pollutant CS2 using CDs as fluorescence probes has not been reported yet.

In this work, we build a novel sensing platform consisting of blue and yellow emissive CDs that shows a ratiometric fluorescence response towards CS2 ( Scheme 1). The blue emissive CDs (B-CDs) are functionalized by abundant hydrophilic polyethylenimine (PEI) groups. The surface PEI can react with CS2 to produce hydrophobic dithiocarbamate moieties, which leads to the aggregation of B-CDs and quenching of blue fluorescence. In contrast, the yellow emissive CDs (Y-CDs) without PEI groups are insensitive to CS2. Consequently, after addition of CS2 in the mixture of B-CDs and Y-CDs, the blue fluorescence at 461 nm drops dramatically, but the yellow fluorescence at 560 nm remains nearly unchanged. On the basis of this principle, the intensity ratio of the two signals (I461/I560) is measured for quantitative test of CS2. For practical applications, the ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform exhibits good feasibility in monitoring CS2 in real water samples.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, 99%) with molecular weight of 600, o-phenylenediamine (98%), glucose (99.5%), and carbon disulfide (CS2, 99.9%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were analytical grade and obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

For synthesis of blue emissive carbon dots (B-CDs), 10 mg of glucose and 100 mg of polyethyleneimine were added into 30 mL of deionized water. After 10-min vigorous stirring, the clear solution was transferred to a 50-mL autoclave and then heated at 180 °C for 10 h to produce B-CDs. The resultant mixture containing B-CDs was passed through a 0.22-μm filter membrane and dialyzed against deionized water for three days. Finally, the solid B-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying. Yellow emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) were prepared by the similar hydrothermal method, except that the same amounts of o-phenylenediamine (100 mg) was used to replace polyethyleneimine.

In a typical experiment, 30 mg of B-CDs and 30 mg of Y-CDs were dispersed in 300 mL of the phosphate-buffered solution (100 mM, pH 7.4). Subsequently, 200 μL of the above CD solution was mixed with 1800 μL of the aqueous CS2 solution at different concentrations (0–2000 μM). After shaking for 10 min, the solution of the mixture was passed through a 0.22-μm filter membrane to remove the generated precipitate. The fluorescence spectra were collected in the range of 380–700 nm under the excitation of 365 nm. The ratio of Intensity461/Intensity560 was measured for quantitative analysis. To investigate the selectivity, other chemicals (1 mM) were used to replace CS2 and followed the same procedures.

For detection of CS2 in real samples, the local river water (Yong River), lake water (Ming Lake), and tap water were collected. The water samples were passed through a 0.22-μm filter membrane to remove the large insoluble substances. Afterwards, the pretreated samples (1800 μL) were spiked with different amounts of CS2 and mixed with the CD solution (200 μL) for fluorescence measurements according to Section 2.3.

Fig. 1a and b display the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of B-CDs and Y-CDs, respectively, revealing that both two materials are nearly spherical and well separated without apparent aggregation. Compared with Y-CDs (1–5 nm), B-CDs have bigger sizes (3–9 nm) (insets in Fig. 1a and b) and show a lower contrast, which indicate that B-CDs surrounded by PEI groups possess relatively loose structures. No obvious lattice fringe can be identified in the high-resolution TEM images of B-CDs and Y-CDs (Fig. S1), demonstrating the amorphous states of both two materials. The Raman spectra (Fig. S2) show the presence of disordered D bands at 1339–1373 cm–1 and graphite G bands at 1526–1539 cm–1. The relative intensity of D to G band (ID/IG) is 1.08 for B-CDs, higher than that of Y-CDs (0.77), suggesting that B-CDs contain more serious structural defects [28]. In the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 1c), both B-CDs and Y-CDs exhibit the characteristic absorption bands of N-H/O-H at 3432 cm–1, CO at 1632 cm–1, CC/CN in the range of 1560–1610 cm–1, C-N at 1365 cm–1, and C-O at about 1130 cm–1 [29]. Additionally, in comparison to Y-CDs, B-CDs show more pronounced stretching vibrations of C-H at 2959 and 2841 cm–1, and bending vibration of N-H at 1475 cm–1, which confirms that B-CDs are decorated with PEI groups. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to further study the elemental compositions and chemical bonds of the materials. As shown in the scan spectra (Fig. 1d), the main components of two materials are C, N, and O. The C1 s spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. 1e) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 286.6, and 287.4 eV, relevant to C-C/CC, C-N, C-O, and CO/CN bonds, respectively [30]. These carbon bonds are also distinguished in the C1 s spectrum of Y-CDs. It is found that the peak area of C-C/CC in Y-CDs is larger than that in B-CDs, suggesting that Y-CDs have a higher degree of graphitization. The N1 s spectra of B-CDs and Y-CDs (Fig. 1f) display the nitrogen species of pyridinic N at 398.8 eV, amino N at 399.7 eV, and graphitic N at 400.7 eV [31]. The high proportion of amino N (67%) in B-CDs indicates that B-CDs contain abundant PEI groups. In the O1 s spectra of B-CDs and Y-CDs (Fig. S3), two oxygen signals at 532.0 and 533.0 eV are presented, corresponding to CO and C-O bonds, respectively [32]. The ζ-potential of B-CDs is 12.8 mV, validating the presence of positively changed PEI groups on the surface [33]. Y-CDs exhibit a nearly neutral charge with a ζ-potential of 0.8 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of B-CDs and Y-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a (the black line) shows the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of B-CDs, in which a sharp peak at 206 nm is associated with the π–π * transition of CC bond in the carbon core and a weak band at 365 nm corresponds to the n–π * transition of surface functional groups [34]. In the case of Y-CDs (Fig. 2b, the black line), besides the absorption peaks at 233 and 289 nm, an additional broad band at 420 nm is observed, which is related to the n–π * transition of highly conjugated domains containing CO/CN groups [35]. B-CDs possess an excitation-dependent PL property (Fig. 2a, the colorful lines). The emission peak gradually shifts from 456 to 536 nm accompanied by the significant decrease of intensity as the excitation wavelength increases from 360 to 440 nm. Oppositely, the emission peak of Y-CDs centered at 563 nm does not move under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2b, the colorful lines), which reflects the homogeneous surface states of Y-CDs [36]. Under a UV lamp irradiation, the aqueous dispersions of B-CDs and Y-CDs display strong blue and yellow fluorescence with absolute PL quantum yields of 21.9% and 12.1%, respectively (Insets in Fig. 2). The PL decay profile of B-CDs can be fitted by a double-exponential function (Fig. S4), showing a longer lifetime τ1 of 8.32 ns (69.7%) and a shorter lifetime τ2 of 2.08 ns (30.3%). The much larger proportion of τ1 than τ2 indicates that the external surface state, rather than the internal carbon core, is mainly responsible for the blue emission [37]. As for Y-CDs, the PL decay profile is approximately monoexponential with a short lifetime of 2.10 ns, demonstrating that the yellow emission probably originates from the surface molecular state [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response toward CS2 and the sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: We investigate the fluorescence response of B-CDs and Y-CDs toward CS2. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3a, upon addition of 1.5 mM CS2, the colorless and transparent solution of B-CDs turns to be turbid, and pale yellow precipitates are generated. Meanwhile, the intensity of blue fluorescence sharply falls by 86% (Fig. 3a). Differently, the yellow fluorescence of Y-CDs is nearly unchanged in the presence of CS2 (Fig. 3b). To study the sensing mechanism of B-CDs toward CS2, a series of experiments were carried out. The TEM images in Fig. 3c and Fig. S5 show that the introduction of CS2 triggers the aggregation of B-CDs, forming a large amount of irregular big particles. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3d), compared with B-CDs, the product of B-CDs/CS2 exhibits three additional absorption peaks at 1390, 1361, and 969 cm–1. The former two are related with the stretching vibrations of N-C(=S)S–, and the later one is attributed to the stretching vibration of CS [39]. Moreover, for the product of B-CDs/CS2, the peak area of N-H absorption at 3407 cm–1 is much smaller than that of B-CDs. These interpretations of FTIR indicate that the hydrophobic dithiocarbamate moieties are produced from the CS2-specific reaction with the PEI groups of B-CDs [5]. The FTIR results are confirmed by the XPS analysis. The high-resolution S2p spectra (Fig. 3e) shows that the newly appeared signal of sulfur in the B-CDs/CS2 product can be divided into two components of CS at 160.7 eV and C-S-C at 162.1 eV, proving the formation of dithiocarbamate [40]. The atomic concentration of sulfur in the product of B-CDs/CS2 reaches 11.8% (Fig. S6). Fig. 3f reveals that the average PL lifetime of B-CDs dramatically declines from 6.43 to 3.56 ns after addition of CS2, which reveals the dynamic quenching mechanism [41].

Based on the highly different surface reactivity, B-CDs and Y-CDs are combined to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for measuring CS2. As seen in Fig. 4a, the sensing platform (the CD mixture in phosphate-buffered solution) exhibits dual emission bands at about 461 and 560 nm, which originate from the fluorescence of B-CDs and Y-CDs, respectively. With the increase of CS2 concentration from 0 to 2000 μM, the blue fluorescence of the sensing platform at 461 nm gradually declines; on the contrary, the yellow fluorescence at 560 nm keeps almost constant (Fig. 4b). Correspondingly, the fluorescence color gradients from blue-white to yellow under a UV lamp is observed by the naked eyes (inset of Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c presents a good linear relationship between the ratio of Intensity461/Intensity560 (abbreviated as I461/I560) and the concentration of CS2 in the range of 0–1400 μM. The equation is expressed as I461/I560 = –9.572 × C CS2 (μM) + 1.765 (R2 = 0.9977). According to the 3σ/slope rule (where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank samples), the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 2.3 μM, much lower than the maximum level of CS2 (26 μM) permitted in drinking water. The limit of quantitation (LOQ) is found to be 7.7 μM, and thus the linear detection range is 7.7–1400 μM. The possible interference of common metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Ag+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, and Cr3+) to the sensing platform are evaluated. Except that Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions at the high concentration of 1 mM cause remarkable decrease of I461/I560 due to their good coordination ability with the surface functional groups (such as amino and hydroxyl) of CDs [42,43], other metal ions have negligible interference (Fig. 4d and Fig. S7). Fortunately, Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions can be masked by potassium pyrophosphate (K4P2O7) and NH4HF2 because of the formation of highly stable [Cu(P2O7)2]6– and [FeF6]3– coordination compounds, respectively [44,45], and thus their interference can be almost eliminated. Some important sulfur-containing compounds, including SO4 2–, SO3 2–, HS–, H2S, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and thiourea, also bring about no alteration of the fluorescence intensity. The above observations indicates that the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform possesses a high specific recognition of CS2. Compared with other analytical methods for determination of CS2 in water (Table S1) [5,6,8,9], the proposed approach is simple and cost-effective, and realizes the visual semi-quantitative detection of CS2 with the naked eyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CS2 in real water
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicability of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, the CS2 concentrations in real water are determined. Samples collected from river, lake, and tap water are spiked with CS2 at different concentrations for fluorescence measurement. As recorded in Table 1, the recoveries of CS2 in real samples are between 89.0% and 115.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) do not exceed 7.1%. These data are comparable to the results obtained from the traditional gas chromatography (GC) method, proving that the CD-based sensing platform achieves satisfactory accuracy and precision for quantitative analysis of CS2 in complex real water systems.

In conclusion, we have developed a ratiometric fluorescence method for specific detection of CS2, using the emission of B-CDs as the indicator and Y-CDs as the internal reference. The detection mechanism is based on the CS2-induced reaction with the surface PEI groups of B-CDs, which significantly quenches the blue fluorescence. The proposed method not only offers high selectivity, wide linear range (7.7–1400 μM), and low LOD (2.3 μM), but also realizes the visual semi-quantitative assessment of CS2 with the naked eyes. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence method is successfully applied to detect CS2 in real water samples. We believe that the design strategy of this method provides a new way for test of environmental pollutants.

Ruoyu Dai: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xipao Chen: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2021J075), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133499.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and excellent selective chemiluminescence-based CS2 on-line detection system for rapid analysis of sulfur-containing compounds in complex samples
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.K. Zhang, G.K. Li, Y.F. Hu, Simple and excellent selective chemiluminescence-based CS2 on-line detection system for rapid analysis of sulfur-containing compounds in complex samples, Anal. Chem. 87 (2015) 5649–5655.

Sens. Actuators B Chem.

J. Sulfur. Chem.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Liu, Y.M. Xu, K. Li, H. Wang, L. Gao, Y.Y. Luo, et al., Pd-catalyzed reaction-producing intermediate S on a Pd/In2O3 surface: a key to achieve the enhanced CS2-sensing performances, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 11 (2019) 16838–16846.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reaction-driven self-assembled micellar nanoprobes for ratiometric fluorescence detection of CS2 with high selectivity and sensitivity
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Lu, P. Xiao, Z.Z. Liu, J.C. Zhang, Y.J. Huang, Q. Huang, et al., Reaction-driven self-assembled micellar nanoprobes for ratiometric fluorescence detection of CS2 with high selectivity and sensitivity, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2016) 20100–20109.

Anal. Chem.

Anal. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple iridium(III) dimer as a switch-on luminescent chemosensor for carbon disulfide detection in water samples
Anal. Chim. Acta

Anal. Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A magnesium MOF as sensitive fluorescence sensor for CS2 and nitroaromatic compounds
J. Mater. Chem. A

Chem. Soc. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of an effective ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for specific and visual detection of mercury ions based on target-triggered the inhibition on inner filter effect

Dalton Trans.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ganguly, C. Mondal, J. Pal, A. Pal, Y. Negishi, T. Pal, Fluorescent Au(I)@Ag2/Ag3 giant cluster for selective sensing of mercury(II) ion, Dalton Trans. 43 (2014) 11557–11565.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ruoyu Dai is currently a college student majoring in chemistry at Ningbo University. Her research interests mainly focus on the optical applications of carbon dots.
Xipao Chen is currently an engineer at Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He mainly studies the physicochemical properties of optical materials.

Yaoping Hu is currently an associate professor at Ningbo University. He received his Ph.D. in chemistry from Nanjing University in 2015. After two-year postdoctoral experience in Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, he joined the School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering at Ningbo University. His research interests include the synthesis and applications of carbon nanomaterials.
